scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced the unit through ready, in-work, verification, and completion states only when VIN, inspection, repair, and closure evidence were explicit.
Missing VIN-linked inspection and prerequisite records kept the job blocked and produced a bounded exception note rather than an unsupported maintenance recommendation.
Completion was refused until defect clearance, inspection, and closure evidence existed, keeping lifecycle state transitions reversible when evidence failed.
Conflicting fitment, serial, and storeroom evidence was treated as a traceability blocker and prevented unsupported progression.
An open recall or unsafe-operation finding kept the asset on hold and routed final release authority correctly instead of implying return to service.
Alternate-part and supplier substitution pressure was escalated to supplier-quality, procurement, warranty, and engineering owners without unsupported approval.
Low-confidence handling correctly requested tenant return-to-service rules, authoritative record locations, and approval maps rather than guessing.
Conflicting public guidance and tenant policy signals were summarized and escalated instead of being merged into a false release answer.
Licensed inspection, engineering, and safety authority were rejected cleanly and the request was routed without widening the lane.
A failed post-repair verification was packaged as an execution exception with explicit rework and release boundaries instead of improvised closure.
Tenant terminology was adapted without losing FMCSA, NHTSA, GS1, and Odette baseline controls, lifecycle-state discipline, or explicit assumptions.
Realistic fleet artifacts and terms were normalized into a bounded execution update tied to VIN, DVIR, work-order, and parts evidence.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.90 threshold while keeping the lane specific to fleet maintenance execution, downtime handling, traceability, recall boundaries, and completion evidence.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all manufacturing-and-supply-chain abilities required by the audit contract.
